text: The surgeon begins the procedure by marking the visceral peritoneum from the ileum to the mesenteric area, using the superior mesenteric vein as a reference point.
The surgeon dissects the superior mesenteric vein below the ileocolic vessels, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon locates the superior mesenteric vein and performs a D-free lymphadenectomy, removing the lymphoadipose tissue covering the anterior surface of the vein, including the surgical trunk of Guillot in the specimen.
The assistant maintains traction on the ileocolic pedicle as the surgeon enters the embryological plane, separating the mesocolon from the underlying retroperitoneal structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the mesocolon, progressing laterally and cranially, separating it from the retroperitoneal structures.
The surgeon dissects the ileocolic pedicle after liberating the entire right mesocolon from the retroperitoneal structures, ensuring clear separation of these anatomical components.
The surgeon encounters the ileocolic vein, followed by the ileocolic artery, which is observed to cross either in front of or behind the superior mesenteric vein during the dissection.
The surgeon performs a central ligation of the ileocolic vein and artery, isolating these vessels from surrounding tissue within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the surgical trunk of Guillot on the anterior surface of the superior mesenteric vein, carefully separating the mesocolon from surrounding structures.
The surgeon completes the separation of the duodenum and pancreas from the mesocolon by sectioning the ileocolic vessels, following the embryological plane of Fredet.
A gauze is placed at the level of the middle colic vessels, serving as a reference point for the cranial approach during the dissection.
The surgeon sections the gastrocolic ligament, gaining access to the epiploic transcavity.
The surgeon uses the right gastroepiploic vessels as a reference point during the dissection, focusing on their anatomical location and relation to surrounding structures.
The surgeon includes a portion of the ligament in the surgical specimen, indicating its removal as part of the procedure.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, sectioning the collohepatic and right colophrenic ligaments to release the hepatic flexure from its attachments.

The surgeon follows the cranial approach to the middle colic vessels, carefully separating the right gastroglipoietic vessels from the transverse mesocolon, tracing their path until they enter the gastrocolic trunk of Henle.
A gauze is placed between the two dissection areas to maintain separation during the procedure.
The surgeon creates a window at the inferior pancreatic border to separate the transverse mesocolon from its posterior attachments, facilitating a safe approach to the middle colic vessels.
The surgeon performs vascular dissection in the area surrounding the middle colic vessels, utilizing the vascular region to facilitate separation of the vessels from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs a D-free lymphadenectomy along the anterior surface of the superior mesenteric vein, carefully dissecting lymphatic tissue in this region.
The surgeon dissects the gastrocolic trunk of Henle, carefully identifying each of its branches within the surgical field.
The surgeon sections the right superior colic vein, which, along with the middle colic vein, is observed draining into the gastrocolic trunk of Henle.
The surgeon performs dissection and central ligation of the middle colic artery, focusing on the vascular structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the lymphadenectomy by performing the central ligation of the middle colic vein, visible adjacent to the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon completes the immobilization of the transverse mesocolon by carefully sectioning the attachments to the pancreas, ensuring the mesocolon is free from pancreatic connections.
The video shows a general view of the surgical area after completing a D-free lymphadenectomy during an extended right hemicolectomy, with visible anatomical structures and surgical field exposed.
The superior mesenteric vein is fully exposed as the lymphoadipose tissue encircling it is included in the surgical specimen.
The surgeon sections the transverse colon after confirming proper vascularization using indocyanine green fluorescence.

The surgeon completes the procedure by performing an intracorporeal isoperistaltic lateral-lateral mechanical ileocolic anastomosis. The enterotomy is closed using a barbed suture, ensuring secure closure of the intestinal incision.
